data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Soaring To New All-Time Highs
data.description:
Article Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) reported strong quarterly earnings results on Thursday, showing once again that it is a major AI beneficiary. A dividend increase rounded out the good news -- not surprisingly, investors reacted positively, with TSM's shares jumping to a new all-time high. Since the stock's valuation is far from high while growth should remain strong in 2026 and beyond, I would not be surprised to see Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company rise even further in the future.

Past Coverage

I have written about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in October 2025, when I covered the company's Q3 earnings release. I was bullish on TSM back then, which worked out well so far -- since my Buy rating was published, TSM has returned 17%, versus a return of around 5% for the broad market over the same time frame. With TSM announcing its Q4 earnings results on Thursday morning, it is time for me to update my views on the company.

What Happened?

On Thursday morning, prior to the market's open, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited announced its Q4 earnings results. As we can see in the following screencap, those were pretty good:

Not only did Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company show very nice business growth, but it also outperformed expectations, delivering a double beat versus Wall Street estimates -- this was the 11th such double beat in a row, showing analysts continue to underestimate this chip giant.

The market reacted positively to this news -- with the announced dividend hike likely also playing a role -- and sent TSM's shares to a new all-time high of just north of $350. Over the last year, TSM is now up more than 70%, and has generated a double-digit return in 2026 alone.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company: A Major AI Winner

Artificial Intelligence has been a major investment theme for a couple of years now, and yet, one of the biggest winners from this trend is still not getting as much attention as it deserves -- the manufacturer that enables all of it, TSM. Without Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), and many more semiconductor companies would not be able to sell any AI chips to the hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), or Amazon.com (AMZN). AI chip, whether GPUs from the likes of Nvidia or TPUs from Alphabet, wouldn't be possible without foundries -- and as the biggest and most technologically advanced foundry in the world, TSM naturally plays a huge role.

The huge AI tailwinds on TSM's business were once again visible in the company's most recent quarterly earnings results: The company's revenue growth of a little more than 25% in US Dollars was driven by advanced chip sales, such as those used in AI data centers, to a significant degree -- 3nm process chips made up 24% of overall revenue in 2025, versus just 18% in 2024. Likewise, the still relatively modern 5nm process saw a larger revenue contribution as well, while older processes became less important.

Looking at the different product categories for which Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company sells its chips, high-performance computing, of course, was the most important growth driver once again -- in 2025, HPC revenues jumped by 48%, with automotive revenues rising by an also very nice 34% over the last year, although from a much lower absolute level. We can assume that a large portion of this growth in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's high-performance computing revenue came from higher sales to Nvidia, which remains the AI chip leader by far.

While the above-average growth rate in new, high-end process and AI chips makes TSM more dependent on those categories over time, it's still nice that its older processes remain major cash cows for the company on top of that -- providing some diversification and cash that can be reinvested in higher-growth areas.

Since most of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's customers are extremely dependent on TSM as a vendor, TSM has massive pricing power -- and the company isn't afraid to use it. Over the last couple of years, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company has repeatedly raised the prices for its products, especially for its high-end processes like 3nm and 5nm. This allowed the company to grow its gross margin nicely over time, which, of course, is a major profit growth contributor -- higher margins thanks to price increases mean that more money flows through to the bottom line, all else equal. During the most recent quarter, basically everything moved in the right direction:

Note: These numbers are in NT$, not US$, which is why the year-over-year growth rate is slightly different, but the trends are the same.

We see that price increases that boosted TSM's gross margin turned a ~21% revenue growth rate into a ~27% gross profit growth rate, but things don't end there. Operating leverage, thanks to operating costs, such as for administration and marketing, growing less than gross profit, made TSM's operating income grow even more than its gross profit -- a ~21% revenue increase was thus turned into a ~33% operating profit increase, with earnings per share growing even slightly faster than that. I like it a lot when a company is able to turn an already attractive business growth rate into an even better earnings growth rate, as this makes achieving a high earnings growth rate easier, and since it proves that the company's fundamentals, including its pricing power, cost controls, etc., are strong.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing: A Good Value Despite Nice Share Price Gains

In US$, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company grew its earnings per share by 40% during the fourth quarter, which is excellent -- and yet, shares aren't really expensive: Based on current estimates for 2026, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company trades at 26x net earnings, which is far from an expensive valuation. When we consider that analysts have a very clear track record of underestimating TSM, I think it's fair to assume that actual results will be at least slightly better than what is currently predicted, which is why I think that the "true" earnings multiple for this year is likely closer to 24-25, not 26.

This is also a rather undemanding valuation compared to how many other AI stocks are trading: Nvidia trades at 25x 2026's earnings (Nvidia's 2026 ends in January 2027, though), AMD trades at 34x 2026's earnings, Broadcom trades at 34x 2026's earnings as well (its 2026 ends in October 2026), and so on. Since all of these companies are extremely dependent on TSM, I don't think TSM should necessarily trade below the average here. After all, if TSM's growth were to slow down, that would only happen if its customers see a growth slowdown as well. And if TSM were to be targeted in a potentially escalating Taiwan conflict, that would impact its customers massively as well -- so I don't think it makes sense that TSM trades with a major geopolitical risk discount, while its customers trade as if that same geopolitical risk wouldn't affect them.

While Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company traded at even lower valuations in the past, I think it's still far from expensive here, considering its excellent market position and strong growth -- both in the recent past and in the future. TSM's guidance for the current quarter, for example, calls for revenues of around US$35.2 billion, plus or minus $600 million. At the midpoint of the guidance range, that would mean an increase of 36% versus one year earlier -- an even better growth rate than during the most recent quarter. At least in the near term, there's thus no growth slowdown at TSM at all.

Takeaway

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited remains one of the biggest winners of the AI theme, showing excellent business growth and strong pricing power thanks to its market dominance.

Shares were cheaper at times in the past, but they don't look expensive at all. With growth most likely remaining strong in 2026, I think TSM stock is still a good investment today -- the 20% dividend increase is just icing on the cake.

data.title: EPI: A Niche Indian ETF That Emphasizes Quantum Of Earnings
data.description:
Introduction to the WisdomTree India Earnings ETF

The WisdomTree India Earnings ETF (EPI) which is issued by the New York-based ETF specialist and asset management firm- WisdomTree Investments, has been in existence since February 2008, during which it has amassed total AUM [assets under management] of $2.7B. To own EPI, investors need to shed out an expense ratio of 0.84%. This ETF does not pay any dividends.

How Is EPI Built?

EPI is a niche play on the Indian equity scene; we say niche because it passively tracks an index called the WisdomTree India Earnings Index [WIEI], which unlike most indices, doesn’t weigh its holdings on the basis of their market-cap or share prices, but rather on earnings (earnings here refers to reported net income over a 12-month trailing basis).

Before we expand on this, note that all constituents who make it for consideration to this index need to have generated at least $5M worth of reported net income over a 12-month time frame, and have a price-to-earnings ratio of at least 2x.

Constituents that have a higher threshold of absolute trailing twelve-month net income are well-positioned to garner a higher weight in this index, but weighting isn’t limited to earnings alone. WIEI also incorporates something called an “Investability Weighting Factor” that is multiplied by the reported net income, to ensure that they don’t load up on highly profitable Indian shares that are close to hitting foreign eligibility limits. This second facet is crucial because the Indian regulatory authorities have imposed certain caps on how many shares of a company foreign institutional investors [FII] are permitted to own. So, if we have a constituent with the highest absolute profits that could see it earn the highest weight, but whose FII cap is almost breached, EPI’s weight towards this won’t be as high.

What Are the Notable Characteristics of EPI’s Portfolio?

EPI covers close to 550 stocks, but don’t expect this portfolio’s holdings to be static over the years. Since individual stock weighting takes place on the basis of the quantum of net income (which as we’ll highlight later has its risks), you’re bound to see a lot of fluctuations within a year’s time frame (reconstitution takes place annually every September). To get more specific, this is a portfolio that witnessed annual churn of 30% last year, which means three of every 10 stocks were bought and sold within a year.

The stocks that make it to EPI are also required to be listed on the Bombay Stock Exchange [BSE], so ADRs [American Depositary Receipts] of Indian stocks won’t be considered. For those not familiar with ADRs

EPI’s tracking index may not be market-cap weighted, but given that it emphasizes quantum of earnings, naturally the largest businesses are better positioned to come out on top. You thus have a portfolio that is heavily weighted towards Indian giant and large caps, which take up over 85% of the total portfolio. Investors should also note that during the screening period, prospective constituents are required to have a minimum market-cap of at least $200M (across a 6-month time frame), which will naturally eradicate a good chunk of micro-caps (micro-cap exposure is less than 0.5% of the portfolio).

Stylistically, EPI tilts mostly towards large-cap hybrid stocks (48% of the portfolio) which typically offer the best of growth and value characteristics (relatively cheap earnings and sales multiples, with relatively strong sales and earnings growth, relatively solid dividend payouts, and relatively strong price momentum across months).

From a sector aspect, EPI offers coverage to 11 different sectors but note that the financial sector alone accounts for roughly one-fourth of the holdings. This could potentially have been higher, but EPI’s tracking index caps sector weights at a maximum of 25%. Interestingly enough, the cap for the real estate sector alone is a lot tighter at just 15% (at this juncture, EPI’s real estate exposure is the lowest at less than 1%).

What Are the Risks Associated With EPI?

EPI weights its portfolio holdings on the basis of reported net income, which may not be the best gauge of the operating performance of a company. We say this because reported net income tends to include quite a few non cash items, and can also be skewed by one-off effects that are unlikely to repeat across many years (EPI only looks at trailing earnings over a 12-month period). So, it’s quite possible that you come across a stock that has made it to EPI because in the most recent year they sold a part of a business and recorded a gain, or maybe received a boost from reversal of certain impairments, etc. Also, some would argue that building a portfolio on “quantum” of net profit may be an inferior gauge to net profit margins (the degree of profit generated for every dollar of sales) or net profit growth over many years. All in all, one could perhaps say that by pursuing EPI, one runs the risks of following a product with a flawed methodology.

EPI is also not a cost-efficient ETF, both by general ETF standards, as well as Indian ETF standards. For context, it is priced at 0.84%, when the median ETF expense ratio is a lot lower at 0.5%. And within the Indian landscape, there are around a dozen different unleveraged alternatives with an expense ratio range of 0.19% to 0.86%. Only one unleveraged Indian ETF (INQQ) is priced more than EPI at 0.86%, while you also have actively managed ETFs like INDE that can be picked up at a cheaper rate (0.79%).

Most of these Indian ETFs also pay distributions, and while EPI also used to do so on a quarterly basis, it has stopped distributing since 2024, thus preventing it from attracting the interest of income investors.

Then, despite EPI being a passively managed ETF, it is questionable if EPI is doing a great job of mirroring its target index in pursuing profitable Indian companies. We say this because EPI’s tracking errors across different durations, is much wider than what ETFs in general experience (7-9%) and suggests that it is following a different path to the index.

Who Is EPI ETF For?

EPI is meant for those who want a wide pool of businesses (over 500 stocks, which is a wider coverage than what most Indian ETFs offer) that are currently generating the greatest quantum of earnings from what is one of the fastest growing regions of the world. For further context note that over two decades ago, India’s share of global GDP was only 1.6%, and now it has more than doubled, with India likely to emerge as the second-largest economy in the world by 2038.

Indian equity ETFs are generally not noted for being priced at cheaper earnings valuation multiples than the globe (because of the growth premium) and are usually priced at par, or above US stocks. However, since EPI is a product that is built and weighted on the quantum of earnings, it comes across as one of the rare Indian ETFs whose earnings valuations are not prohibitive (put another way, unlike market-cap weighted products, EPI isn’t doubling-down on stocks with high market-cap relative to their earnings). To elaborate, EPI can be picked up at an earnings multiple of a little over 16x, which works out to a 13% discount over global stocks and a 28% discount over US stocks.

Note also how EPI’s P/E ratio has consistently lagged the multiple of the MSCI India Index, which is covered by the iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA) which with almost $10B of AUM represents the most popular Indian ETF around. All of this suggests that value-conscious investors who want to garner access to the high growth Indian story will likely view EPI quite favorably.

EPI will also appeal to those who dislike major concentration effects that tend to crop-up with market-cap weighted products. EPI does not allow any of its stocks to cross an individual weight of over 24%, and all stocks with individual weights of over 5% are prohibited from jointly accounting for 50% of the index. As a result of all this note that EPI’s top 10 stocks account for less than 37% of the total portfolio (the median ETF tends to see top 10 exposure of 42%). Also recollect that EPI caps its sector exposure at 25%.

What Alternatives Are There For EPI?

Besides EPI, we’d also like to highlight the credentials of the iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA), and the Frankin FTSE India ETF (FLIN) both of which are currently the only two ETFs that are larger than EPI (in terms of AUM). INDA is of course far bigger than EPI (despite getting listed four years later) and FLIN with an AUM approaching almost $10B (its popularity can also be noted in the tightness of the bid-ask spreads seen in this counter at just 0.01%).

Like EPI, both these products are also passively managed but track two different indices; FLIN tracks the FTSE India RIC Capped Index, and INDA tracks the MSCI India Index. Unlike EPI’s tracking index which is weighted on earnings and the foreign investors investability factor, these two indices are market-cap weighted. Note that both these portfolios provide no exposure to small or mid-caps (unlike EPI), while INDA barely covers even mid-caps.

EPI still offers the widest reach covering almost double the number of stocks as FLIN, and over 3x more than INDA. All portfolios tilt towards the financial sector, but as noted earlier EPI’s share will always be capped at 25%.

FLIN stands out for its highly efficient expense ratio of just 0.19%, while also being the only ETF that currently offers a yield (its distributions are made twice a year).

As noted earlier, EPI’s earnings emphasis ensures that it is the cheapest portfolio from an earnings multiple basis.

Summary

EPI which is not the most cost-efficient Indian ETF around, offers wide coverage to fast growing Indian stocks at reasonable valuations. EPI has stopped making distributions since 2024, and its tracking errors are on the higher side.

This article answers these three main questions about EPI:

  • What are the key features of the EPI portfolio?
  • What type of investor could use EPI, and what risks are associated with EPI?
  • How does EPI compare to other major India-focused ETFs?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: AI Is Endless, And TSMC's Upside Still Isn't Fully Priced In
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), aka TSMC, reported Q4 '25 earnings on Wednesday after the bell, and the stock shot up over 6%.

Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) are also up around 2%, while ASML Holding (ASML) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) were both up another 6% and 5%, respectively, in sympathy with TSMC. The company’s report was highly anticipated, as so much of the sentiment around AI demand hinged on TSMC’s numbers and commentary. The results largely put concerns to rest. So, without further delay, here are the key takeaways.

Earnings rundown

TSMC delivered another strong quarter, comfortably beating expectations on both the top and bottom line, with diluted EPS up 35% year over year. Revenue came in at NT$1.046 trillion (approximately $33.7 billion), up 20% year over year and ahead of consensus, while net income came in at NT$505.7 billion, also above consensus. This was TSMC’s eighth consecutive quarter of year-over-year profit as demand for AI chips remains strong. Gross margins also remained strong at 62.3%, while operating margin came in at 54% and net profit margin came in at 48.3%.

Looking forward, the company expects revenue to be in the range of $34.6 billion to $35.8 billion, a 4% quarter-over-quarter growth, and around 38% year-over-year growth. Gross profit margin is expected to expand to be in the range of 63% to 65%, while operating margin is expected to be unchanged for the most part at 54% to 56% during the same period.

For Q4 '25, 3-nanometer made up 28% of total wafer revenue, while 5-nanometer came in at 35%, 7-nanometer at 14%. The bulk of TSMC’s revenue comes from advanced nodes, as they remained the backbone of TSMC’s growth profile, with “7-nanometer and more advanced technologies, accounted for 77% of total wafer revenue,” and 74% for FY25, up from 69% in FY24, according to the press release.

For the quarter, high-performance computing (HPC), including AI and 5G, made up 55% of revenue, while smartphones came in at 32%.

As the company pushes further into cutting-edge manufacturing, TSMC has already entered mass production of its 2-nanometer process and plans to accelerate the ramp this year.

This strategic shift toward more advanced nodes is being matched by a sharp increase in investment. TSMC expects Capex to reach $52 to $56 billion in 2026, up from $40.9 billion in 2025, with 70% to 80% allocated to advanced process technologies.

"In 2025, we spend US$40.9 billion, as compared to US$29.8 billion in 2024, as we begin to raise our level of capital spending in anticipation of the growth that will follow in the future years,"

said Wendell Huang on the earnings call.

Risks and concerns

While consumer electronics face potential headwinds from memory shortages and higher component prices, management acknowledged the risk. The company CEO, C.C. Wei, was fairly transparent about the memory issue, and during the earnings call, said that

As for PC or the smartphone, to tell the truth, we expect higher memory price. So we expect the unit growth will be very minimal. But for TSMC, we did not feel our customer change their behavior. And we look at it and then we found out that we supply most of the high-end smartphones. The high-end smartphone is less sensitive to the memory price. So the demand is still strong. Using one sentence, I'd like to say we still try very hard to narrow the gap. We have to supply a lot of wafers to them also.

Tariff uncertainty was another point of focus, and Wei said that

entering 2026, we understand there are uncertainties and risk from the potential impact of tariff policies and rising component prices, especially in consumer-related and price-sensitive end market segment.

As capacity remains right and demand outpaces supply, the company announced the purchase of

a second land in Arizona…We are going to expand many fabs over there and this giga-fab cluster can help us to improve the productivity, to lower down the cost and to serve our customers in the U.S. better.

I look at this as a two birds, one stone kind of move: TSMC is playing nice with the U.S. government and at the same time addressing long-term capacity, cost, and geopolitical risk. Expanding its Arizona footprint strengthens supply-chain resilience for U.S. customers, but more importantly, it positions TSMC as a strategic partner amid rising pressure for onshore semiconductor manufacturing.

Investment thesis and past coverage

I initiated the stock with a Buy back in late August of 2025, and it is up around 44% since then, outperforming the market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up around 8.3% since then. At the time, I saw that the company’s global expansion and U.S. investments help it mitigate geopolitical risks and position it for long-term growth despite tariff uncertainties. That stands more true now than ever.

Also, at the time of my coverage, Intel (INTC) was getting a lot of attention post the government stake, but I didn’t see their business competing with that of TSMC anytime soon. That also remains true, despite Intel's latest moves.

I’m maintaining my long-term thesis on TSMC and think that the absence of competition at the leading edge remains underappreciated. Despite renewed optimism around Intel’s foundry ambitions and ongoing geopolitical pressure to diversify manufacturing, no other player is currently matching TSMC’s scale, yield, and execution at advanced nodes. The company’s dominance in 3nm today and its accelerating 2nm ramp reinforce a competitive moat that is widening.

TSMC continues to function as the clearest barometer for AI infrastructure demand, and the setup into 2026 remains compelling. Capex is on the rise and is being deployed into the highest-return segments of the market.

The broader takeaway is that TSMC remains in a position of strength. Memory prices and tariff uncertainty may weigh on certain consumer segments, but they have not meaningfully altered customer behavior, particularly at the high end, where TSMC’s exposure is greatest. With AI, HPC, and leading-edge smartphones driving the mix, demand continues to outpace supply, giving TSMC visibility for FY26 and beyond.

According to a Digitimes report back in mid-December of last year,

It is estimated that by the end of 2026, TSMC's CoWoS monthly capacity will reach 125,000 wafers, representing annual growth of more than 70%. Meanwhile, the combined average monthly CoWoS-like capacity of ASE, SPIL, and Amkor is expected to reach around 40,000 wafers. This marks multiple-fold growth compared with 2024.

CoWoS capacity is a key gauge of real demand. If demand continues to ramp, TSMC will need to scale advanced packaging capacity accordingly, as higher AI demand requires expanded CoWoS throughput. To keep a long story short, it’s not a matter of guessing who might show up, but building the infrastructure to support volumes that are already visible in the order book.

Wei, when asked about whether demand is real or not, said that he’s

“also very nervous about it. You bet because we have to invest.. If we didn't do it carefully, and that would be big disaster to TSMC for sure. So of course, I spend a lot of time in the last 3, 4 months talking to my customer and end customers' customer…they show me the evidence that the AI really help their business…So no doubt…I believe in my point of view, the AI is real, not only real, it's starting to grow into our daily life..But I look at the AI, it looks like it's going to be like an endless, I mean, that for many years to come.”

Valuation and what’s next

TSMC is deserving of a premium valuation for all the reasons I mentioned. I’m pinning TSMC against Intel.

Intel appears attractive on select valuation metrics, more specifically those tied to sales and book value. At 4.6x forward sales and 2x forward book, both below the sector median, and a forward non-GAAP P/E of 143x, while forward EV/EBIT exceeds 100x, I see a disconnect. Growth metrics reinforce the latter: revenue is expected to decline both year over year and forward, EBITDA growth remains behind the sector, and forward EPS growth is sharply negative.

By contrast, TSMC trades at 11.7x forward sales, 9.4x forward book, and 23x forward operating cash flow, all well above sector medians. On the surface, those multiples look demanding. But on the earnings-based valuation, you get a different story. TSMC’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 31x and forward EV/EBITDA of 16x are far more reasonable given the company’s growth profile and margin structure. With a 37% year-over-year revenue growth and 30% forward revenue growth, alongside 30%+ forward EBITDA and EBIT growth and mid-30% forward EPS growth, TSMC remains more attractive in my books.

So, while Intel may look cheap on certain valuation metrics, those mask a shaky earnings profile and ongoing operational risk. The numbers favor TSMC, in my opinion.

Going forward, while sentiment has been overwhelmingly moving AI stocks and the market, earnings like this reinforce that the AI trade is alive and well. The stock is now protected by the EMA21 at $315 in the near term and the EMA50 at $302 in the mid-term, while long-term support remains to be in the $257 range. With RSI in overbought territory, near term upside might be capped, but the long-term trajectory remains bullish. I’m maintaining TSMC with a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: No Match For Starlink
data.description:
Setting The Stage

AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (NASDAQ:AST) is building the world’s first space-based cellular broadband network that is designed to connect directly to standard, unmodified smartphones.

The use case is simple.

As Ray Myers explained in his post,

“Probably most of us have been in a situation where we couldn’t make a phone call, send a message, or access the internet because of a weak connection.

Conventional wireless networks connect us using cell tower base stations. Each tower has a limited coverage radius in which devices can connect for their data to be transferred through the broader network. This is a very capital-intensive system that cost trillions of dollars to build and requires billions to maintain and expand.

This makes it unprofitable to build and maintain connections in remote areas with low economic activity. Furthermore, this network is vulnerable to disruptions during natural disasters.”

ASTS is looking to solve the problem by building and launching satellites in space that will allow users to access 4G and 5G broadband using their mobile devices without any extra hardware.

They recently launched their Blue Bird 6 satellite, which management calls the “largest commercial communications array ever deployed in low earth orbit." that will enable peak data rates of up to 120 Mbps directly to standard, unmodified mobile devices, supporting voice, full data, and video applications from space.

Also, note that these new generation Blue Bird satellites are thrice the size and have 10 times the capacity of its six satellites currently in orbit, given the company’s innovation with its custom silicon chip AST 5000.

The launch news has helped the stock propel past $100 per share, marking a new all-time high. But, with the bulk of the revenues projected to show up only after FY26 and no profitability, the stock is trading at a jaw-dropping forward P/S ratio of 452. This leaves little room for execution error, along with investors wondering whether there is any further upside left in the stock price at current levels.

Making Sense Of ASTS's Forward Revenue Projections

Before we go any deeper, let us first start by discussing the two business segments of ASTS, which include 1) Government and 2) Commercial.

Starting with the Government segment, ASTS is a prime contractor for the US government to provide resilient, high-bandwidth communications for mission-critical applications. In fact, the $14.74M in revenue that the company generated in its Q3 FY25 earnings was primarily driven by US government contract milestones, with management confident that they will continue to ramp their US government efforts as we plan for large contracts going forward.

Turning our attention to the Commercial segment, it is important to note that ASTS does not sell its services directly to consumers. Instead, it acts as a wholesale satellite connectivity provider for MNOs (Mobile Network Operators), where they integrate ASTS’s space-based broadband into their existing plans as an “add-on” with a revenue share model.

At the moment, ASTS has signed agreements with over 50 MNOs with over 3B subscribers and $1B in aggregate contracted revenue commitments, as they expanded their relationship with 2 additional operators during the quarter. This includes a 10-year agreement with the STC Group, which is a leading mobile network operator partner in the Gulf region, that involves a prepayment of $175M made at the end of 2025. Meanwhile, Verizon also agreed to expand their strategic partnership that positions ASTS to target 100% of geographical coverage in the US.

In order to fuel the company’s ambitions to deploy intermittent nationwide service in the US in early 2026 and prepare for continued service later in 2026, along with plans for activations in Canada, Japan, Saudi Arabia, and the UK, management is targeting a launch of 45-60 Blue Bird satellites by the end of 2026, enabling ASTS to begin earning service revenues from its agreements with mobile partners.

To note, ASTS currently has 7 satellites in orbit, with management laying out that BlueBird 8 to 19 are in various stages of production and that they are on schedule to complete 40 satellites by early 2026, bringing them to BlueBird 46. In terms of their launch cadence, management has announced orbital launches every 1–2 months throughout 2026, which will get them to their target of 45–60 satellites to support continuous global cellular broadband service by the end of 2026.

Given the progress, management has reiterated their H2 FY25 revenue guidance of $50-75M. However, the real inflection in revenue is projected to come in FY26 and beyond, where revenue is expected to grow 300%+, followed by growth in the 100%+ range over the next two years to surpass over $2.5B in total revenue. This will be driven by a combination of the successful conversion of their revenue commitments with MNOs based on the completion of infrastructure and/or service activation milestones, along with its recurring revenue share model with MNOs based on customer/subscriber usage and strength in government contracts.

Having said that, these revenue projections are not set in stone. As we can see, the revenue projections below have been revised downward substantially over the last 6M given the delays in their satellite launches in 2025 that pushed revenue growth further out.

Don't Underestimate The Competition From Starlink

On January 7, Scotiabank sent out a note saying that ASTS faces an uphill battle to compete with SpaceX’s Starlink, with a recommendation to sell the stock and a price target of $45.60 per share.

According to the analyst, investors will have to wait until 2028 or 2029 for tangible equity-free cash flow generation while competing with Starlink, which has become the “absolute leader” on the satellite fixed-broadband side in 150 countries.

Having said that, one of the primary differences between ASTS and Starlink is that the former is a direct-to-device connectivity partner to MNOs, while the latter is primarily a direct-to-consumer internet company that requires a dedicated dish terminal and router with thousands of smaller satellites that provide high-speed internet service.

In essence, therefore, while ASTS partners with MNOs to expand cellular coverage in “dead zones” with a revenue share model, Starlink competes with the internet divisions of MNOs with a direct subscription model.

However, with the acquisition of spectrum licenses from EchoStar for $17B, Starlink is catching up on ASTS’s direct-to-device business, as the spectrum purchase will now allow SpaceX to start building and deploying upgraded, laser-connected satellites that the company said will expand the cell network's capacity by "more than 100 times."

“Gwynne Shotwell, President and COO of SpaceX, also said the deal will help the company "end mobile dead zones around the world. With exclusive spectrum, SpaceX will develop next-generation Starlink Direct to Cell satellites, which will have a step change in performance and enable us to enhance coverage for customers wherever they are in the world."- Reuters

Note that in addition to ASTS’s core partnership with MNOs to utilize their licensed spectrum, it too has acquired 60 MHz of global S-band spectrum priority rights and 45 MHz of Ligado’s L-band spectrum to reduce its reliance on third-party carrier spectrum while simultaneously expanding its capacity and speed to deliver reliable service across regions.

While ASTS’s existing revenue commitments with MNOs are a validation of its technological innovation along with the distribution and cost advantages that these partnerships provide, Starlink is undoubtedly ahead in its commercialization phase with its verticalized manufacturing strategy, where it has 9M subscribers globally along with a significantly larger constellation size.

Assessing ASTS Investment Thesis & Conclusions

Like I discussed earlier, ASTS is projected to see significant revenue growth towards the latter half of FY26 into FY27 and beyond. However, all of that is dependent on the successful launch of its planned 45–60 satellites in FY26. If we look at the past history, the company did experience several delays with their satellite launches in 2025, which caused consensus to drive down their forward revenue growth expectations.

It is hard to predict whether ASTS will experience similar delays with their planned launch of 45–60 satellites in FY26. The stock is currently trading at a forward P/S ratio of 454. Assuming no share dilution from current levels, the forward P/S ratio will shrink to just approximately 12, based on current FY28 revenue growth estimates.

However, in this case, that would not be a fair assumption, as the company has issued new shares to raise capital for its satellite manufacturing and deployment, with shares outstanding growing over 75% YoY in the latest quarter.

Meanwhile, when we look at the strength of the balance sheet, ASTS has over $3.2B in cash and liquidity at the end of Q3, which management believes is sufficient to manufacture and launch over 100 satellites with an estimated cost of $21-23M per satellite.

At the moment, the company is naturally operating at a loss. However, based on consensus estimates, it should start earning positive earnings per share by FY27, when revenue growth will start to outpace operating expenses. However, similar to forward revenue projections, earnings per share estimates can also be revised higher or lower based on the schedule of satellite launch and how efficiently it is able to translate its backlog into revenue growth.

However, assuming its FY27 earnings per share estimates, the company is trading at a forward PE ratio of 38.

If you are wondering whether it is too high or too low a number, the bigger question is how big of an uncertainty is tied to the projected FY28 earnings per share figure.

According to me, the risk is significant when it comes to the execution and timing, along with the growing competitive force from Starlink.

As a result, I will remain on the sidelines at the moment while recommending new investors wait for a normalization of the stock price to its 10W levels on the chart, which corresponds to a $77 per share price target, to initiate a small speculative position on the stock with better risk-reward than at current levels.

data.title: Boston Scientific Acquiring Penumbra Was The Logical Next Step
data.description:
Already one of the best growth stories in med-tech, Boston Scientific (BSX) has once again turned to M&A to not only bolster its growth profile but also fill one of the relatively few obvious gaps in the portfolio. Acquiring Penumbra (PEN) will be expensive and dilutive up front, but the deal adds billions of dollars in potential revenue (with a strong growth rate and healthy profitability), and I believe Boston Scientific can accelerate Penumbra’s growth above and beyond what the company could have done on its own.

I like the deal, and with Boston Scientific shares selling off a bit since my last update on the company, the shares are getting more interesting as a potential investment even though the multiple is still quite robust.

The Deal

Boston Scientific and Penumbra announced on January 15 that they’d reached an agreement whereby Boston Scientific will acquire Penumbra for $374/share. This is a nearly 20% premium to Penumbra’s prior close and about 6% above my estimate of Penumbra’s fair value.

The deal is being structured as a mixed cash/stock offering, with investors having the option to choose between $374/share in cash or 3.8721 shares of BSX stock. The deal is collared for a 73% cash/27% stock mix, so while investor preferences will be respected if possible, investors may not get all of the cash or shares that they want.

Paying around 9x sales, it’s not surprising that this deal will be dilutive to BSX in the first year, with a high single-digit return in or by year five.

There was no discussion on the call about any sort of breakup fee, and the deal proxy has not been filed yet as of this writing, but I would expect a non-prohibitive break-up fee. That said, for the price that BSX is paying, there really aren’t many companies that could theoretically step up and offer a higher bid if they wanted to (Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) theoretically could, but I don’t expect it).

What BSX Is Getting

In acquiring Penumbra, Boston Scientific is getting the best independent mechanical thrombectomy platform, and there are arguments to be made that Penumbra is in a stronger position today than Stryker’s (SYK) Inari, which Stryker paid a little over 8x forward revenue to acquire about a year ago.

As per Penumbra management, the company addresses a mechanical thrombectomy market that currently encompasses about 1.25M potential procedures in the U.S. today, and most of those markets are less than 20% penetrated. What’s more, I would argue that management’s assessments of the addressable markets for pulmonary embolism (157K patients) and venous/DVT (351K patients) are conservative, and more addressable procedures will be unlocked with subsequent clinical trials, product development, and physician experience.

While the ASPs vary by procedure (and sometimes within procedures), I believe Penumbra adds something in the neighborhood of $10B in addressable revenue, with mid-teens growth likely over at least the next five years and quite possibly double-digit growth over the next decade. Though I am focusing primarily on Penumbra’s mechanical thrombectomy business here, there is a meaningful embolism business as well, including growth opportunities like SwiftPac in middle meningeal artery (or MMA) treatment, which I discussed in my last article on Penumbra.

Moreover, I believe BSX could be in a position to expand and accelerate Penumbra’s growth and market development. BSX has a much larger global presence and can accelerate Penumbra's growth outside the United States. Moreover, while clinical trials take the time they take, Boston Scientific has the resources to accelerate clinical trial development to further expand the data book on Penumbra’s technology and devices, something that should accelerate adoption in the years to come (likely 5-plus years from now).

The Logic Of The Deal

This is a straightforward deal as far as I’m concerned, and I actually called out Penumbra as a likely M&A target for Boston Scientific in that last BSX article. BSX already has a strong presence in many related markets (it’s the leader in peripheral vascular) and mechanical thrombectomy was a clear gap for a company that has a sizable vascular and peripheral interventions business, particularly after the 2024 acquisition of Silk Road.

Buying Penumbra is clearly expensive, but mechanical thrombectomy will now sit alongside electrophysiology (specifically the Farapulse pulsed field ablation catheter) as one of the premier addressed markets with roughly $10B in served market revenue opportunities and double-digit market growth (to that end, Penumbra preannounced over 21% revenue growth for Q4, well ahead of the Street expectation of mid-teens growth).

With multiple countries into which Boston Scientific can expand product availability, as well as large addressable markets that can be further penetrated with time, product development, and clinical work, this is a pretty classic BSX opportunity, and one that is probably in no later than the second or third inning for investors who prefer sports metaphors.

Doing some very quick remodeling, I see this deal as ultimately net-neutral to BSX, assuming no revenue or cost synergies (BSX management called out around $200M in combined synergies in year three). While letting Penumbra remain more or less independent may limit some of the post-deal operating synergies, I do nevertheless see opportunities on the sourcing, manufacturing, and sales/distribution side even with a more hands-off structure. Likewise, I do see meaningful potential revenue synergies, and I think this deal ultimately will boost the DCF-based fair value by around 5%.

There’s also value here above and beyond the DCF value. Med-tech stocks are valued in no small part on profitable growth, and Penumbra will enhance the three- and five-year revenue growth rates, and that could drive further expansion of the multiple. One caveat here, though, is that BSX was already trading in pretty rarified air with respect to how med-tech stocks have been historically valued, and even allowing that there aren’t many examples like BSX (large, profitable companies with sustained double-digit revenue growth potential), there is an ongoing risk of derating back to more normal valuation standards.

The Bottom Line

For Penumbra shareholders, I think this is a good deal, and I expect it to go through without much drama. Choosing between cash and stock has a lot to do with individual preferences and needs, particularly how you feel about BSX’s valuation. For BSX shareholders, I like this deal, and I think it will add value over time. What’s more, with the shares selling off, this is a name that is getting increasingly interesting to me as a potential buy even with my concerns about the risk of derating.

data.title: Economic Forecast 2026: Growth Will Defy Slowdown Fears
data.description:
The economic outlook defies the slowdown narrative so common these days. A job boom is not in the cards, but solid growth is the most likely path for 2026. After that, the United States will probably enjoy moderate gains in economic activity.

The economy showed strong growth in the most recent data on gross domestic product. GDP is our broadest measure and thus deserves our closest attention. In the third quarter, the inflation-adjusted growth rate rose to 4.3%. The average gain since 2000 has been 2.2%, so we were well above average. Fourth quarter GDP might be softer because of the government shutdown, but so far the data are coming in hot. The Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank tracks incoming components of GDP. Based on this partial data, fourth quarter looks like a 5.1% increase. (Their estimate is updated with every data release, and they caution that it’s not a forecast.)

Layoff announcements worried many analysts, and 2025 certainly exceeded anything since the pandemic. But December’s announcements were very low. Actual filings to start receiving unemployment insurance benefits are very low—we’re in a layoff drought right now. The flip side, though, is that hiring is also quite low.

Demographics dictate low job growth. The home-grown population of working age is about level. Job gains in recent years have come from immigrants. But with the Trump administration clamping down on entry and increasing deportations, net immigration into the country is very low. 2025 saw the least job growth since the pandemic, but likely also the lowest increase in the working-age population.

With low immigration, the unemployment rate should provide a good look at how the job market is doing. At 4.4% in December, it’s up 0.3 percentage points in the past 12 months. But this information comes from a telephone survey, which is then extrapolated to the total population. Fewer people are answering telephone surveys in general. Immigrants are much less likely to take a government survey call now, so we may be missing a high-employment segment of the population. And extrapolation to the entire population is difficult without accurate data on just how many people have entered and left the country. Official estimates show about zero net immigration in 2020 (the last year of the first Trump administration and also the year of the pandemic), rising to nearly three million in 2024 (the last year of the Biden administration). Last year’s population estimate has not yet been released, but it very likely will come in closer to zero than to three million people.

Total output is the product of the labor exerted and its productivity—output per hour worked. Productivity’s trend growth has been about two percent per year. Artificial intelligence will boost productivity, but its rollout has been gradual so far. Programmers and customer service staff are more productive per hour worked, and we have isolated gains in many other industries. But we do not yet see enough AI-driven productivity growth to move the aggregate economy much.

So the supply side constrains our growth potential.

On the demand side, consumers have income and show a willingness to spend it. Pessimists talk about a K-shaped economy, with some people’s incomes increasing (the rising portion of the letter), while others have declining income (the downward-sloping portion). The data show that low-income workers have actually, on average, received pay raises in excess of inflation. Taken as a whole, the past six years have seen lower-paid workers receive larger raises than others. With their incomes up more than inflation, there’s no reason for pessimism on their account.

Businesses have been spending more for equipment and development of intellectual property. They have been doing much less construction, even with data centers added into the picture. Still, their total capital expenditures have increased.

Government spending has also grown, as has net exports, so the demand side looks good going forward.

The evidence discussed so far is retrospective. But the economy normally grows. Productivity—output per hour worked—has a long history of improving, which fuels spending capacity for the overall economy. Labor productivity gains turn into wage gains, as companies compete for workers. To tell a slowdown story, one needs a change. The slower immigration into the U.S. is one such change that must be accounted for, but it’s not going to send the economy into recession.

Federal Reserve policy is nearly neutral. The neutral interest rate, dubbed R* (r-star) by us economists, is never seen directly but instead estimated from historical relationships. Most likely it is slightly contractionary, but not hugely so. The Fed seems not inclined to further interest rate cuts given the stubborn inflation rate. Thus monetary policy will likely have little impact on economic changes in the coming year.

Tariffs have not yet shown up in domestic prices, though they seem likely to in 2026 and beyond. The tariff impact on domestic manufacturers of more expensive raw and intermediate materials will be negative, but only slightly so relative to the entire economy, which includes substantial service activity.

Business plans based on 2025 averages will work well for most companies in 2026. All organizations (including non-profits and local governments) should sketch out contingency plans for alternative economic environments. Most important is the recession plan, focused on how to pay the bills if revenue drops. Also valuable is an upside contingency plan, outlining how to serve more customers if demand increases.

Doom and gloom always sells better than Pollyanna—but Pollyanna turns out to be more accurate, on average, in the long run. So plan on good times, while protecting your organization from the worst consequences if doom, or gloom, come along.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BlackRock Is Built To Compound Through Any Market
data.description:
BlackRock’s (BLK) latest earnings back up a story that’s been building for a while: this is a company still on the front foot. When I called it a Buy at $1,150, I saw a steady business that could compound for years, powered by ETFs, big moves into private markets, and a growing edge in tech. Now, after a 5% dip to about $1,092, BlackRock hasn’t skipped a beat. If anything, I didn’t give them enough credit for just how fast things are coming together.

Record Inflows and Growth From Multiple Engines

Quarter after quarter, money keeps pouring in. This past year set a new record ($698 billion in net new money, with $342 billion just in the last three months). They now manage $14 trillion. Growth from new business hit 12% in the fourth quarter, and it’s not all just ETFs. Private markets, active management, outsourcing, and cash products are all kicking in. CEO Larry Fink summed it up well:

“We ended the year with back-to-back quarters of double-digit organic base fee growth, including 12% in the fourth quarter. BlackRock enters 2026 with accelerating momentum across our entire platform, coming off the strongest year and quarter of net inflows in our history.”

BlackRock isn’t just big. It’s built to handle just about anything. The price, at 20 times this year’s earnings and just under 18 times next year’s, isn’t wild for a business showing this kind of steady, high-margin growth. Profits are staying around 45% even after all the big purchases and investments. You can actually see how profits could rise, and the stock could get a higher multiple if the company gets more out of these deals. With GIP, HPS, and Preqin all under the roof, they’re aiming to squeeze more earnings out of tech, data, and private market deals. Raising the dividend by 10% and adding 7 million shares to the buyback shows management’s confidence the cash is coming in.

A Diversified Platform With Broad Reach

BlackRock spreads its bets across a huge mix of products and clients. Right now, almost 40% of assets are in ETFs, about 23% in bonds and 16% in alternatives (with a lot more focus now on higher-fee, stickier areas like private and active funds). The GIP and HPS deals brought in roughly $260 billion in infrastructure and private credit, and buying Preqin gave them a top-notch data business. These aren’t just random buys. They’re trying to turn BlackRock into the place where both big institutions and wealth managers come for pretty much everything. Management made it clear: this isn’t just about getting bigger; it’s about actually boosting growth and profits.

Asset managers are splitting in two directions. Low-fee index funds for the masses and high-fee, high-value stuff for big clients who want more than just average returns. BlackRock is the rare player that can do both. Some rivals have scale but not the tech edge. Others are strong in private assets but don’t have BlackRock’s reach or tech. The firm’s tech advantage (especially with Aladdin and now Preqin) makes it tough for clients to leave. And while bigger market worries haven’t stopped BlackRock from bringing in new business or keeping its high-fee products, most smaller players are feeling the squeeze.

Financial Results Show Strong Profits and Cash Flow

The big news from today’s report: full-year revenue jumped 19% to $24.2 billion. Profits were up, too. Operating income rose 18%, and EPS hit $48.09, even with all the new shares from deals. Profit margins are holding up at 44%. The $698 billion in net inflows is a record, and new business is driving up organic base fees by about 9% over the past year, jumping to 12% in the fourth quarter. Free cash flow is strong, and with $12.6 billion in cash on hand, the balance sheet is rock-solid. There’s plenty of cash to invest or buy back shares.

Zooming in on the latest quarter, BlackRock's Q4 numbers were just as impressive. Revenue for the fourth quarter hit $7 billion, up from $5.7 billion in the same period last year. Adjusted EPS for Q4 came in at $13.16, well ahead of expectations, and GAAP EPS was $7.16. Operating income (as adjusted) reached $2.85 billion for the quarter, with operating margins still right around 45%. Net inflows in the fourth quarter alone totaled $342 billion. These results show that BlackRock's growth isn't just an annual story; it's happening quarter by quarter, with strong momentum heading into 2026.

The stock isn’t a bargain, but for a business with BlackRock’s growth and profitability, the valuation doesn’t look stretched compared with the rest of the group. If you use a 21 times earnings multiple on 2027’s consensus earnings, you land in the $1,300 range, so there’s about a 19% upside, plus a 2% dividend that’s likely to grow. Since BlackRock has shown it can do this, I think there’s room for the stock to move up, especially if the company pulls more out of its tech and private markets deals.

Most investors agree BlackRock is top-tier for bringing in new money, but they’re not in a hurry to buy more because of choppy markets and the distraction of recent deals. The dip since October looks like more of a market thing or just some people cashing out profits, not a problem with BlackRock itself. Management’s move to raise the dividend and buy back more shares shows they really believe in the business. Headlines are mostly positive, and there hasn’t been any really bad news.

The risks? Maybe BlackRock struggles to blend in all these new businesses, or maybe new growth slows down. If there’s a major market crash or the stock falls for no real reason, there’s always a chance of a setback. But the business is more balanced and stronger than ever, with more money coming in from high-fee, high-margin stuff. If new growth misses by 15%, that might cut earnings by 10-12%. That’s mostly balanced out by buybacks and all the cash they make.

Outlook Favors Continued Growth and Margin Expansion

For the next couple years, I see BlackRock pushing 10-12% growth from new business, with profits likely to go up as the company gets bigger and more efficient. Revenue could hit $27-30 billion and EPS might reach $62 or more. The main things to keep an eye on are how well they blend in their new deals, how much new money they bring in each quarter and whether profits grow faster than expenses as the business gets bigger. If these happen, the stock will probably go up. If not, the current price isn’t too risky.

In a bad case, growth slows and profits don’t improve, so the stock stays where it is. The middle ground gets you to $1,300 and if everything clicks, $1,400 isn’t out of the question. It all comes down to delivering on tech, private markets and capital return.

I’m sticking with my Buy call on BlackRock after this quarter. Right now, BlackRock gives you growth, stability and the chance for more gains (which makes it worth buying at these prices). What could trip this up? A big mistake blending in new businesses, slower growth, or the stock price falling for no real reason. But if they keep on the path they’re on, there’s a lot to like here.

data.title: U.S. Economy: What It Would Take To Bring Back Home Affordability
data.description:
As the midterm elections are approaching, more and more rhetoric around housing affordability is starting to flood the airwaves. The POTUS just announced two major efforts to address this large issue. The first was potentially barring large institutional names like Blackstone (BX) from purchasing additional single-family homes. This might help marginally on the cost front in some cities where institutional buyers have solid footprints, like Atlanta, Dallas, and Charlotte.

However, institutional players only account for under two percent of single-family home buyers. 'Mom and Pop' investors who own between one and five single-family homes make up nearly 90% of investors in this sector.

The second policy push from the administration was the announcement that Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) will be directed to buy $200 billion in mortgage bonds to bring down mortgage rates. The news pushed mortgage rates down just below 6% last Friday, their lowest levels in three years. This decline also triggered a 40% surge in weekly mortgage refinancing applications as well.

Realtor.com just published new analysis on what it would take to increase home affordability so these metrics were more within historical norms. The numbers are quite shocking:

  • Mortgage rates falling to 2.65%, down from 6.15% currently, or

  • Incomes rising 56% to a median of $132,171, up from $84,763 currently, or

  • Home prices falling 35% to a median of $273,000, down from $418,000 last year.

Let's take a look at each of these statistics individually. Obviously, absent massive QE by the Federal Reserve involving purchasing large amounts of mortgage bonds, mortgage rates are most likely to just trend lower (hopefully) for the rest of the year. Mortgage rates are in the same range as they occupied at the start of the 21st century before the Great Financial Crisis. This 'normalization' should continue outside another crisis.

Average wages did not keep up with inflation from 2021 to 2023 thanks to the surge of prices that spiked the CPI up to a peak of 9.1% in June 2022. Over the past two years, wages have been rising slightly faster than inflation. The December BLS jobs report showed a 3.8% annual increase in annual wages. The December CPI reading showed inflation rose 2.7% from the same period a year ago.

A good chunk of improving housing affordability is likely to come from falling home prices. Following Covid, the Federal Reserve increased the money supply by roughly 40% over two years. Congress pumped trillions in Covid-related and other stimulus into the economy as well. This helped trigger a surge in asset prices, including stocks and real estate. In many regions of the country, especially in the Sun Belt states, average home prices rose 40%, 50%, 60%, or even 70%.

Several of these previous hot markets, like Austin, TX, are already in bear markets with price declines of more than 20% from their peaks.

The number of existing home sales has also tanked since the Federal Reserve started to raise rates in March of 2022. Existing home sales in 2025 were at their lowest levels since 1995 (when the population was 20% lower).

It is likely prices for existing homes will fall in the years ahead. The average cost of a new home fell to below that for an existing home for the first time in memory this summer.

Obviously, this is untenable. New homes have new roofs, appliances, and HVAC systems. Therefore, their maintenance and insurance costs are significantly lower than existing homes in the same area. Barring a surge of new home prices (unlikely), the way this divergence will be resolved over the medium term is existing home prices will need to fall.

It is understandable why the administration is so focused on housing affordability in front of the upcoming mid-term election in early November. This is a key driver of consumer sentiment, which is in a pessimistic state of mind right now. Roughly one-sixth of GDP is also driven by housing-related activity.

In addition, this is important on the inflation front. The 'shelter component' is the biggest part of the much-watched monthly CPI reading. The CPI component 'Owners Equivalent of Rent,' or OER, makes up 26.4% of the weighting of the all-items CPI. OER also accounts for 33% of core CPI and 44% of core services CPI.

I continue to be underweight on homebuilding stocks and equities that are housing-related like Home Depot (HD), within my portfolio. My view continues to be that home affordability needs to improve 30% to 40% before the moribund housing sector can revive. This will likely happen over many years via some combination of falling home prices, rising wages, and hopefully a decline in average mortgage rates.

Investors will have a much better picture of the health of the housing market by the end of the second quarter. Data points around the all-important spring selling season should be available by then. With more homeowners not locked into under 4% mortgages, the spring could see additional inventory come to market. In addition, after roughly four years of taxpayer-financed largesse, the Covid-related FHA mortgage loss mitigation programs were largely ended on October 1st.

There are approximately eight million FHA mortgages. These are low money down (typically a 3.5% down payment) mortgages that can quickly go 'underwater' in a housing downturn. The scary part is that even with these extraordinary efforts, over 10% of these mortgages were delinquent at the end of 2024.

Given the length of the foreclosure process, a surge of FHA foreclosures should start to be evident by end of Q2.

And as was noted by housing analyst Melody Wright late in December

609,000 borrowers who were current on payments in October became delinquent in November, marking the largest single-month inflow since May 2020. Rolls from 30- to 60-day and 60- to 90-day delinquency bands also increased sharply."

That tells me the changes to the FHA mortgage mitigation programs are already having ramifications. How extensive those impacts are should become more much apparent in the quarters ahead.

And that concludes my latest look at the housing sector and its impacts on the economy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
